sections.summary: Applies to fresh asparagus shoots (Asparagus officinalis L.) supplied to consumers—excluding asparagus for industrial processing—classifies shoots into four colour groups (white, violet, violet/green, green) and excludes green/violet‑green shoots <3 mm diameter and white/violet shoots <8 mm diameter when packed in uniform bundles or unit packages.

Establishes the standard’s quality provisions: minimum requirements for asparagus (integrity, soundness, cleanliness, freedom from pests/damage, appropriate moisture/odour/appearance, etc.) and a three‑class classification system (Extra, Class I, Class II) with special provisions and tolerances for each class.

Lists minimum requirements applicable to all classes: shoots must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of visible foreign matter and pests/damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, fresh in appearance, practically unbruised, with as‑clean‑as‑possible base cuts, not hollow/split/peeled/broken (small post‑harvest cracks allowed within Section 4.1 tolerances), and developed to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.
States asparagus classification into three classes—'Extra', Class I and Class II—and introduces the detailed criteria that distinguish each class.

'Extra' class requires superior, well‑formed, practically straight shoots with very compact tips, only minimal rust traces removable by peeling, white shoots must be white (small pink tint allowed), green shoots ≥95% green, no woodiness permitted, and base cuts should be square (outer shoots may be beveled up to 1 cm for presentation).

Class I requires good, well‑formed shoots (may be slightly curved) with compact tips, slight rust traces removable by peeling allowed, white shoots may show a faint pink tint, green shoots ≥80% green, no woodiness for white group while other groups may have removable lower‑part woodiness, and base cuts should be square.

Class II covers shoots meeting minimum requirements but not higher classes: they may be less well formed or more curved with slightly open tips, may show rust traces removable by peeling, white tips may be green‑tinted and violet tips slightly green, green shoots must be at least 60% green, shoots may be slightly woody and base cuts may be slightly oblique.
States that asparagus size is determined by shoot length and diameter and introduces the specific length and diameter sizing rules in the following subsections.

Defines length categories and limits for asparagus shoots: long (>17 cm), short (12–17 cm), tips (<12 cm), and Class II arranged (white/violet 12–22 cm; violet/green and green 12–27 cm), with maximum lengths of 22 cm for white/violet and 27 cm for violet/green and green and a maximum 5 cm length variation within firmly bound bundles.

Requires diameter to be measured 2.5 cm from the cut end and sets minimum diameters and allowed variation by colour group and class: for white/violet Extra ≥12 mm (max variation 8 mm), Class I ≥10 mm (max variation 10 mm), Class II ≥8 mm (no uniformity provision); for violet/green and green Extra and I ≥3 mm (max variation 8 mm), Class II ≥3 mm (no uniformity provision).
Permits specified tolerances in each package for asparagus not meeting the declared class, with detailed quality and size tolerance provisions for the different classes and specific deviation limits.

Specifies quality tolerances for each class (Extra, I and II), setting allowable percentages of shoots not meeting the declared class but acceptable under lower-class criteria or specific minor defects.

Allows up to 5% by number or weight of shoots in an 'Extra' class package that do not satisfy Extra requirements but meet Class I (or, exceptionally, are within Class I tolerances) or have slight unscarred cracks after harvesting.

Allows up to 10% by number or weight of shoots in a Class I package that do not satisfy Class I but meet Class II (or, exceptionally, are within Class II tolerances) or have slight unscarred cracks after harvesting.

Permits up to 10% by number or weight in Class II packages of shoots that satisfy neither the class nor minimum requirements (excluding produce unfit for consumption), and additionally allows 10% for hollow shoots or very slight washing cracks with a maximum of 15% hollow shoots per package or bundle.

For all classes allows up to 10% by number or weight of shoots deviating from the indicated size with a maximum length deviation of 1 cm and up to 10% deviating in diameter by a maximum of 2 mm, and in no case shall diameter be less than 3 mm.

Sets presentation requirements covering uniformity (same origin/quality/colour group/size with limited colour allowances), packaging materials and cleanliness, and permitted presentation forms (firmly bound bundles, arranged but not bundled, or prepackaged units).

Requires each package/unit/bundle to be uniform by origin, quality, colour group and size, allows limited cross‑colour inclusions (white packages may contain up to 10% violet in Extra and I and 15% in II; violet/violet‑green/green packages may contain up to 10% of another colour), permits white/violet mixtures in Class II if marked, and requires the visible contents to be representative of the whole.

Requires packaging that protects asparagus using new, clean, food‑grade materials (including recycled food‑grade), allows printed materials only with non‑toxic ink/glue, mandates packages be free of foreign matter, and requires compliance with CAC/RCP 44‑1995.

Permits presentation as firmly bound bundles (outer shoots matching the bundle average in appearance and diameter; Extra class bundles must be equal length; bundles arranged evenly and may be paper‑protected with equal bundle weights within a package), or as arranged but not bundled, or as prepackaged units placed in another package.

Specifies marking and labelling requirements: consumer packages must follow CODEX STAN 1‑1985 plus specific rules, and non‑retail containers must bear legible, indelible particulars (identification, nature, origin, commercial identification and optional inspection mark) with details given in subsequent subsections.

Adds to the general Codex labelling standard for consumer packages by requiring specific provisions, notably that if the produce is not visible the package must be labelled with the product name (and may indicate variety).
States that if the produce is not visible from the outside, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may indicate the variety.

Requires non‑retail containers to bear legible, indelible markings (or include the particulars in accompanying documents) showing identification, nature, origin, commercial identification and optional official inspection mark, as detailed in subsections 6.2.1–6.2.5.
Requires the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher to appear on the package, with an optional identification code.

Requires the product to be labelled 'Asparagus' followed by the colour indication ('white', 'violet', 'violet/green' or 'green') if contents are not visible, and where appropriate the terms 'short', 'tips' or 'mixture white and violet'.
Requires the country of origin to be shown on the package and optionally the district, national, regional or local place name where grown.

For asparagus, commercial identification must show the class; size expressed as minimum and maximum diameters if subject to uniformity rules, or as minimum diameter followed by maximum diameter or the words 'and over' if not; and the number of bundles or unit packages when packed as such.

Where national law requires name and address this must be declared, and if a code mark is used the reference 'packer and/or dispatcher (or equivalent abbreviation)' must be shown in close connection with the code mark.

The produce must comply with the maximum levels in the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CODEX STAN 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Preparation and handling of the produce is recommended to follow relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice (e.g., CAC/RCP 1-1969, CAC/RCP 53-2003) and the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established under CAC/GL 21-1997.
